title: Cloudflare Page Rules and Redirect Chain Depth Analysis (`cloudflare:json`)
dataSources: Cloudflare Logpush JSON delivered to Splunk HEC/S3 with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json` (HTTP requests, firewall, Workers, DNS, Access as configured)
app: Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) + Logpush JSON (`cloudflare:json`)
spl:
index=cdn sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath ClientRequestURI
| spath EdgeResponseStatus
| where EdgeResponseStatus IN (301,302,307,308)
| eval hops=1
| stats sum(hops) as redirects by ClientRequestHost, ClientRequestURI
| where redirects>3
| sort -redirects
description: Redirect loops and excessive hops hurt SEO and mobile latency. HTTP request logs expose chains that configuration UIs hide.
value: Web platform and SEO teams detect redirect loops and excessive hop chains caused by misconfigured Page Rules, preventing crawl-budget waste and mobile latency degradation before search rankings drop.
implementation: Configure Logpush jobs per dataset; route all into a unified `cloudflare:json` sourcetype or use transforms to normalize. Use `spath` for PascalCase fields. Create macros per zone name.
visualization: Sankey or table (from→to status), histogram (hop count).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Web.Web
  by Web.user, Web.url, Web.http_method span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) v2.x on the Search Head. Logpush configured for the **HTTP Requests** dataset with fields: `ClientRequestURI`, `ClientRequestHost`, `EdgeResponseStatus`, `OriginResponseStatus`, `EdgeStartTimestamp`, `ParentRayID` (for subrequests).
• Data routed to `index=cdn` with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json`.
• Page Rules or Bulk Redirects configured in Cloudflare. Page Rules are limited to 3 on Free plans, 20 on Pro, 50 on Business, 125 on Enterprise. Bulk Redirects (newer) have higher limits.
• Understanding: Cloudflare processes Page Rules in order from first to last, and only the first matching rule applies. Redirect chains occur when Rule A redirects to a URL that matches Rule B, which may redirect again.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify HTTP Requests dataset includes redirect-related fields:
```spl
index=cdn sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-1h
| spath EdgeResponseStatus | spath ClientRequestURI | spath ClientRequestHost
| where EdgeResponseStatus>=300 AND EdgeResponseStatus<400
| stats count by EdgeResponseStatus, ClientRequestHost
```
Status 301 (permanent) and 302 (temporary) from the edge indicate Page Rule or Bulk Redirect matches. 303, 307, 308 may also appear. If you see high 301/302 volumes without corresponding origin traffic, Page Rules are the source.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Redirect chain detection:
```spl
index=cdn sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath EdgeResponseStatus | spath ClientRequestURI | spath ClientRequestHost | spath ParentRayID
| where EdgeResponseStatus>=300 AND EdgeResponseStatus<400
| stats count as redirects dc(ClientRequestURI) as unique_uris values(EdgeResponseStatus) as status_codes by ClientRequestHost
| where redirects > 100
| sort - redirects
```

Understanding this SPL: High redirect counts on a single host indicate potential redirect loops or overly broad Page Rules. The `unique_uris` count distinguishes between many different URLs being redirected (broad rule) versus the same URL being redirected repeatedly (loop). If `ParentRayID` is present, the redirect triggered a subrequest that was itself redirected — a chain.

Redirect loop detection:
```spl
index=cdn sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath EdgeResponseStatus | spath ClientRequestURI | spath ClientRequestHost
| where EdgeResponseStatus>=300 AND EdgeResponseStatus<400
| eventstats count as redirect_count by ClientRequestURI, ClientRequestHost
| where redirect_count > 10
| stats count values(EdgeResponseStatus) as codes by ClientRequestURI, ClientRequestHost
| sort - count
| head 20
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Use `curl -sI -L <url>` (follow redirects) on a suspected chain URL. Count the hops and verify each matches a Page Rule or Bulk Redirect in the Cloudflare dashboard.
(b) In Cloudflare: Rules → Page Rules. Review the rule order and identify overlapping match patterns. Test with Cloudflare's "Trace" tool (Enterprise) or the API: `GET /zones/{zone_id}/pagerules`.
(c) Compare redirect volumes in Splunk with Cloudflare's Analytics → Traffic → filter by status code 301/302.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Cloudflare — Redirects & Page Rules"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: "Total redirects (24h)", "Potential loops detected".
• Row 2 — Table: host, URI pattern, redirect count, status codes, unique URIs.
• Row 3 — Timechart: redirect volume by host over 7 days.

Alert: Trigger when the same `ClientRequestURI` generates more than 10 redirects in a 1h window — indicates a loop. Route to the web platform team.

Runbook:
1. **Redirect loop confirmed** — Identify which Page Rules overlap. Common cause: Rule A redirects `http://` to `https://`, Rule B redirects `/old-path` to `/new-path` on `http://`, creating a loop. Fix by reordering rules or making Rule B scheme-agnostic.
2. **High redirect volume after Page Rule change** — A new rule with a broad match pattern (e.g., `*example.com/*`) is catching URLs not intended. Narrow the pattern.
3. **SEO impact** — Redirect chains longer than 2 hops hurt crawl budget. Consolidate to direct 301 redirects.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No 301/302 events in Splunk but redirects work in browser** — The redirect may be happening at the origin, not at the Cloudflare edge. Check `OriginResponseStatus` — if the origin returns 301/302, Cloudflare passes it through. Page Rule redirects show `EdgeResponseStatus=301` with no origin contact.
• **ParentRayID missing** — Not all Logpush configurations include this field. Without it, chain detection is approximate. Request the field in the Logpush job configuration.
• **Redirect counts seem inflated** — Bots and crawlers follow redirects and generate additional requests. Filter `| where BotScore>50` to focus on human traffic (requires Bot Management).
grandmaExplanation: We check that when our website sends visitors from one page to another, it does not accidentally send them in circles or through too many detours.
